2.1.a.info:
An access port is a switch port configured to carry traffic for exactly one VLAN. It is the port type you assign to any endpoint that does not understand VLAN tags — PCs, printers, IP cameras, servers without tagging configured, IoT sensors. From the endpoint's perspective, the cable looks and acts like a plain Ethernet link; the VLAN concept is invisible to it.

How the switch handles frames: On ingress, a frame arrives untagged. The switch assigns (tags) the frame internally with the port's configured access VLAN ID — this tag is used only inside the switch and across any trunks the frame later crosses, never on the wire to the endpoint. On egress, the switch strips the tag before putting the frame on the wire, so the endpoint receives a standard untagged Ethernet frame. This is why Wireshark on a PC connected to an access port never shows 802.1Q headers.

Default behavior and the DTP trap: By default, a Cisco switch port is in dynamic auto or dynamic desirable DTP mode — it will negotiate a trunk if the other side asks. If you only type switchport access vlan 10 without switchport mode access, the port can still form a trunk during negotiation, which breaks the single-VLAN guarantee. Always configure mode access explicitly.

One VLAN rule with one exception: An access port carries one data VLAN. The only legal way to carry a second VLAN on an access port is a voice VLAN for an IP phone — the phone tags voice frames with the voice VLAN, and the PC behind it uses the untagged data VLAN. That special case is covered in its own card.

Verification: show vlan brief lists the access port under its assigned VLAN. show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport shows Administrative Mode = static access, Access Mode VLAN = 10, Operational Mode = static access.

hack.memory: Access port = ONE-lane driveway to ONE neighborhood (VLAN). Tag goes on at the gate (ingress), tag comes off before the car hits the street (egress). The endpoint never sees a tag — that's the whole point. If you forget switchport mode access, DTP may turn your driveway into a highway (trunk) without asking.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, plug a PC into Gi0/1 on a 2960. Configure switchport mode access + switchport access vlan 10. Run show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport and confirm Operational Mode = static access. Now delete both commands and just re-type switchport access vlan 10 — the port defaults back to dynamic auto. Connect to another switch configured switchport mode trunk and watch a trunk form via DTP even though you said 'access vlan.' That one experiment cements why mode access is mandatory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 16-17 (VLANs) walk through access port config explicitly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8 covers access port semantics and DTP interaction. The exam will show a two-line config and ask why a port isn't carrying only VLAN 10 — answer: missing switchport mode access.

2.1.a.info:
switchport mode access is the interface-level command that hardcodes a port into access mode and disables DTP negotiation for trunking. It is the single most important VLAN-related command most engineers forget to type, because omitting it usually still works — until it doesn't.

What it changes: A fresh Cisco switch port defaults to dynamic auto (on most 2960/3560/9200 IOS versions) or dynamic desirable (older images). In those modes, the port sends/accepts DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol) frames and can form a trunk with a neighbor. When you type switchport mode access, the port: (1) stops sending DTP, (2) rejects trunk negotiation, (3) becomes static access — it can only carry one VLAN, whatever switchport access vlan says.

Why it matters for security: Leaving ports in dynamic auto/desirable is the root cause of switch spoofing attacks — an attacker plugs in a device that sends DTP frames claiming to be a trunk neighbor, and the switch happily forms a trunk, giving the attacker access to every VLAN crossing that link. Hardcoding switchport mode access on every user port, and switchport nonegotiate on trunks, eliminates this class of attack.

Paired command — switchport nonegotiate: For defense in depth, some environments also run switchport nonegotiate, which disables DTP frames entirely. Not strictly required on access ports (mode access already ignores DTP), but common on security-hardened configs. On trunks it's more useful because trunk mode alone still sends DTP.

Verification: show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport should show Administrative Mode: static access and Negotiation of Trunking: Off. If you see Administrative Mode = dynamic auto, you forgot the command.

hack.memory: switchport mode access = 'lock the door at access mode, throw away the DTP key.' Default ports will chat with any neighbor and form a trunk — never assume 'I set the access VLAN, I'm safe.' Rule of thumb: every user-facing port gets TWO lines minimum — mode access + access vlan. No exceptions.
hack.practice: Lab this: on Sw1 Gi0/1, leave it default. On Sw2 Gi0/1, switchport mode trunk. Connect them. Run show interfaces trunk on Sw1 — it shows a trunk even though you never configured one. Now switchport mode access on Sw1 Gi0/1 and repeat — the trunk dies. Then try switchport nonegotiate on a trunk and watch DTP frames stop in Wireshark/simulation mode.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 16 introduces the command; Day 19 (DTP and VLAN hopping) explains the attack it prevents. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 8 and 10 cover DTP behavior. Exam will ask 'which command prevents a port from becoming a trunk?' — the answer is switchport mode access (and optionally switchport nonegotiate).

2.1.a.info:
switchport access vlan [id] is the interface-level command that assigns an access port to a specific VLAN. Without it, the port sits in the default VLAN (VLAN 1). With it, every untagged frame entering the port is internally tagged with the configured VLAN ID, and the port only accepts traffic destined for that VLAN.

Auto-creation behavior: If you type switchport access vlan 50 and VLAN 50 does not exist in the switch's VLAN database, the switch creates VLAN 50 automatically with a default name like VLAN0050. This is convenient but also a source of subtle bugs — a typo in the VLAN ID silently creates a ghost VLAN, and your host ends up isolated in a VLAN nothing else uses. Always verify with show vlan brief after assignment.

Typical config block:

interface Gi0/1
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 10
 spanning-tree portfast
 spanning-tree bpduguard enable
 no shutdown

The last two lines are best-practice additions for any edge port: PortFast skips the STP listening/learning delay so the host gets link immediately, and BPDU Guard shuts the port if it ever receives a BPDU (meaning someone plugged a switch into a user port).

Interaction with the VLAN database: Creating a VLAN explicitly with vlan 10 + name Sales in global config is preferred over letting the auto-create happen, because you control the name and it shows up cleanly in documentation. But the port command works either way.

Reverting: no switchport access vlan on the interface puts the port back in VLAN 1.

hack.memory: switchport access vlan 10 = 'stamp every letter entering this mailbox with neighborhood 10.' Auto-creates the VLAN if missing — convenient, but typos create ghost VLANs. Always follow with show vlan brief to verify the port actually landed where you meant.
hack.practice: On a fresh 2960 in Packet Tracer: interface Gi0/1, switchport mode access, switchport access vlan 99. Run show vlan brief — notice VLAN 99 was auto-created with name VLAN0099. Delete it with no vlan 99 and watch Gi0/1 move to an inactive VLAN state. Re-add it with a real name: vlan 99 / name Management. This sequence teaches auto-create, name discipline, and how a port behaves when its assigned VLAN is removed.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 16 covers the command with live examples. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8 emphasizes the auto-create behavior and recommends pre-creating VLANs for cleanliness. Exam sims often ask you to assign a port to a VLAN — type both mode access and access vlan to be safe.

2.1.a.info:
A voice VLAN is a special second VLAN configured on an access port so a Cisco IP phone can carry its voice traffic separately from the PC's data traffic — on the same physical cable and the same switch port. It is the one legal exception to the 'access port carries one VLAN' rule.

The physical topology: PC → cable → back of IP phone → cable → switch port. The IP phone is a 3-port mini-switch internally: one port to the switch, one to the PC, and one for its own voice traffic. When configured correctly, voice frames from the phone leave the phone tagged with the voice VLAN ID (802.1Q), while the PC's data frames pass through the phone untagged and are assigned to the access VLAN by the switch.

Configuration (two lines on the switch port):

interface Gi0/1
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 10      ! data VLAN for PC
 switchport voice vlan 20       ! voice VLAN for phone

How the phone learns its VLAN: The switch announces the voice VLAN ID to the phone via CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol) or LLDP-MED (the IETF standard with Media Endpoint Discovery extension). When the phone boots, it receives the voice VLAN ID in a CDP/LLDP-MED TLV and configures itself to tag its voice traffic with that VLAN. This is why CDP is enabled by default on Cisco switches — disabling it globally breaks voice VLAN auto-discovery unless you switch to LLDP-MED.

QoS benefit: Isolating voice traffic in its own VLAN lets you apply QoS policies (trust DSCP/CoS markings, priority queuing) specifically to voice without affecting data. The phone marks its own traffic with CoS 5 / DSCP EF, and the switch trusts those markings on the voice VLAN only.

Verification: show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport shows both Access Mode VLAN and Voice VLAN lines. If Voice VLAN = none, the config didn't apply. show cdp neighbors should show the IP phone once it boots.

layers: IP phone passes PC frame untagged
IP phone tags its own voice frames VLAN 20
Switch port: data VLAN 10 + voice VLAN 20

hack.memory: Voice VLAN = the ONE legal way to carry two VLANs on an access port. The phone is a mini-switch: tags its voice, leaves PC data untagged. Commands: access vlan 10 (data) + voice vlan 20 (voice). Phone learns voice VLAN from CDP/LLDP-MED — kill CDP and voice auto-config dies.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer supports IP phones. Add a 2960 switch, an IP phone, and a PC. Connect switch → phone → PC. On the switch port: switchport mode access, switchport access vlan 10, switchport voice vlan 20. Create both VLANs. Verify with show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport — you should see both Access VLAN 10 and Voice VLAN 20. Bonus: capture traffic on the trunk side and confirm voice frames are tagged 20 while data frames are untagged.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 16-17 covers voice VLANs. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8 has a dedicated voice VLAN section. Exam will ask 'how does an IP phone learn its voice VLAN?' = CDP or LLDP-MED. Also 'which two VLANs can coexist on an access port?' = one data VLAN + one voice VLAN.

2.1.a.info:
VLAN IDs are 12-bit values in the 802.1Q header, so the total range is 0 to 4095. Of those, 0 and 4095 are reserved (0 = priority-tagged only, no VLAN ID; 4095 = reserved for implementation use and not forwardable). The usable range on Cisco switches is 1 through 4094.

Normal vs extended range: Cisco splits usable VLANs into two tiers:

  • Normal range: 1-1005. Stored in vlan.dat in flash. Propagated by VTP versions 1 and 2. This is the range most exam questions care about.
  • Extended range: 1006-4094. Stored in running-config (not vlan.dat on older images). Propagated only by VTP version 3. Historically required VTP transparent mode on v1/v2.

The reserved block — 1002-1005: These four IDs are hardcoded reservations for legacy media types — 1002 (fddi-default), 1003 (token-ring/trcrf-default), 1004 (fddinet-default), 1005 (trbrf-default). You cannot create, delete, or use them for Ethernet. They exist for VTP compatibility with ancient Token Ring and FDDI deployments. If you type vlan 1002, IOS rejects it.

VLAN 1 is the default VLAN, used by all ports out of the box and by control-plane protocols (CDP, VTP, PAgP, STP for VLAN 1). It exists permanently — no vlan 1 errors out.

Practical advice: In production, stick to the normal range (2-1001) unless you have thousands of VLANs. Use meaningful blocks — e.g., 10-99 for users, 100-199 for servers, 900-999 for infrastructure/management/parking. Document the scheme.

Why 12 bits? The 802.1Q tag is 4 bytes: 16-bit TPID (0x8100) + 3-bit PCP (priority) + 1-bit DEI (drop eligible) + 12-bit VID. The 12 bits give 2^12 = 4096 total values, minus the 2 reserved at the edges = 4094 usable.

hack.memory: VLAN math: 12 bits in the 802.1Q tag → 4096 possible → 4094 usable (0 and 4095 reserved). Normal range 1-1005, extended 1006-4094. The four weird reservations 1002-1005 = 'Token Ring and FDDI ghosts' — they haunt the VLAN database forever. Mnemonic: 'twelve bits of trouble, four thousand doors, four reserved for grandpa's token ring.'
hack.practice: On a real switch, try vlan 4094 — works. vlan 4095 — rejected (reserved). vlan 1002 — rejected (Token Ring reserved). show vlan brief always shows 1, 1002, 1003, 1004, 1005 present by default. Try to delete them: no vlan 1002 — refused. This one minute of CLI trial makes the range rules stick.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 16 introduces the range. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8 covers normal vs extended and the 1002-1005 reservation. Exam hits this every time: 'what is the valid VLAN range?' = 1-4094, 'which VLANs are reserved?' = 1002-1005, 'how many bits in the VLAN ID?' = 12.

2.1.a.info:
show vlan brief is the go-to verification command for VLAN configuration on a switch. It shows every VLAN in the switch's database along with a list of the access ports assigned to each VLAN. It is short, clean, and the first command engineers run when troubleshooting VLAN assignment.

What the output looks like:

VLAN Name                             Status    Ports
---- -------------------------------- --------- -------------------------------
1    default                          active    Gi0/3, Gi0/4, Gi0/5
10   Sales                            active    Gi0/1
20   Engineering                      active    Gi0/2
99   Management                       active
1002 fddi-default                     act/unsup
1003 token-ring-default               act/unsup
1004 fddinet-default                  act/unsup
1005 trnet-default                    act/unsup

The trap that everyone falls for: show vlan brief does NOT show trunk ports. If Gi0/24 is a trunk carrying VLANs 10 and 20, you will not see Gi0/24 listed under VLAN 10 or VLAN 20 in this output — it will appear nowhere. Students troubleshoot a missing port for 10 minutes before realizing the port is a trunk. To see trunk ports and the VLANs they carry, use show interfaces trunk instead.

Status column: active means the VLAN is usable. act/unsup is the Token Ring/FDDI reserved VLANs (active but unsupported on Ethernet). act/lshut means the VLAN has been shut down with shutdown vlan 10 in global config — ports assigned to it lose Layer 2 forwarding for that VLAN.

Filtering variants: show vlan id 10 (detail for one VLAN), show vlan name Sales (by name), show vlan (full output with MTU, etc. — too verbose for normal use).

Exam framing: Expect to be shown a show vlan brief output and asked where a port is assigned, or whether a port is a trunk. If the port is missing from the output entirely, the answer is 'it's a trunk.'

hack.memory: show vlan brief = 'roster of ACCESS ports only.' If a port is missing from the list, it is either shut down or (most commonly) a TRUNK. Trunks live in show interfaces trunk, not here. Exam trick: 'why isn't Gi0/24 in the VLAN 10 list?' = it's a trunk.
hack.practice: Build a switch with VLANs 10, 20, 99 and three access ports, plus a trunk to another switch on Gi0/24. Run show vlan brief — note Gi0/24 is NOT listed anywhere. Run show interfaces trunk — now Gi0/24 appears with its allowed VLAN list. Do this once and you will never lose time on a trunk-invisible question again.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 16 demonstrates the command repeatedly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8 emphasizes the trunk-invisibility quirk. The exam absolutely tests this — multiple practice tests include a show vlan brief output with a trunk deliberately missing.

2.1.a.info:
show interfaces switchport (optionally per-port: show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport) is the most detailed VLAN/trunk verification command on a Cisco switch. It dumps the full Layer 2 operational state for each interface — administrative mode, operational mode, access VLAN, voice VLAN, native VLAN, allowed VLAN list, negotiation status, and more.

Key fields to recognize (from per-port output):

  • Administrative Mode: what you configured (static access / trunk / dynamic auto / dynamic desirable).
  • Operational Mode: what the port actually negotiated to be (static access / trunk / down). If admin says 'dynamic auto' and operational says 'static access,' DTP didn't form a trunk.
  • Access Mode VLAN: the VLAN used when operational mode is access.
  • Trunking Native Mode VLAN: native VLAN if it becomes a trunk (default 1).
  • Administrative Native VLAN tagging: enabled/disabled (for native VLAN tagging).
  • Voice VLAN: none or the configured voice VLAN ID.
  • Negotiation of Trunking: On (DTP running) or Off (nonegotiate or hardcoded).
  • Trunking VLANs Enabled: allowed VLAN list on trunks.

When to use which verification command:

  • show vlan brief — 'where are my access ports?' Quick roster view.
  • show interfaces trunk — 'which trunks exist and what VLANs do they carry?'
  • show interfaces switchport — 'give me EVERYTHING about L2 for this port.' Use when troubleshooting unexpected behavior.

Common troubleshooting use: Port is configured switchport access vlan 10 but frames aren't flowing. Running show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport reveals Operational Mode = trunk (DTP negotiated a trunk because you forgot mode access), Access Mode VLAN is irrelevant because the port isn't in access mode. Fix: switchport mode access.

hack.memory: show interfaces switchport = 'tell me EVERYTHING about this port's L2 brain.' Admin mode = what you typed. Operational mode = what the port ACTUALLY is after negotiation. When they disagree, DTP or a shut interface is to blame. This is the command you run when show vlan brief isn't enough.
hack.practice: On a port, leave mode default and set switchport access vlan 10. Connect to a trunk-mode neighbor. Run show interfaces Gi0/1 switchport: Admin = dynamic auto, Operational = trunk. Now add switchport mode access and re-run: Admin = static access, Operational = static access. Seeing the Admin vs Operational split in real output is the best way to internalize this command.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 16 and 19 (DTP) use this command heavily. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 8 and 10. Exam sims may give you a broken port and ask which command reveals the problem — show interfaces switchport is almost always the right answer because it shows admin vs operational mismatch.

2.1.b.info:
VLAN 1 is the default VLAN on every Cisco switch. Out of the box, every port — access or trunk-capable — is assigned to VLAN 1 until you configure something else. VLAN 1 is also the default native VLAN on 802.1Q trunks, meaning any untagged frame crossing a trunk is assumed to belong to VLAN 1 unless the native VLAN is changed.

Why VLAN 1 exists and can't leave: Several Cisco control-plane protocols are hardcoded to ride VLAN 1 regardless of configuration — CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol), VTP (VLAN Trunking Protocol), PAgP (Port Aggregation Protocol for EtherChannel), DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol), and STP BPDUs for the common instance. The switch needs VLAN 1 to exist to send/receive these messages, so IOS permanently protects it.

The security concern: Because VLAN 1 is universal — every Cisco switch out of the box has it, every trunk carries it, every control-plane protocol lives on it — it's the most predictable attack surface. If an attacker can get a device into VLAN 1 (e.g., by plugging into an unconfigured port), they may be able to snoop control-plane traffic or attempt VLAN hopping via double-tagging (which specifically exploits VLAN 1 as the default native VLAN).

Practical consequences of the default: (1) A switch fresh from the box will put every endpoint in VLAN 1 — instant flat network, every device can talk to every other device at L2. (2) Any unconfigured port on a production switch is a risk — plug in, get on VLAN 1. (3) By default, untagged frames on a trunk are accepted into VLAN 1. (4) The switch's own management SVI is often in VLAN 1 by default on fresh configs (though best practice moves it).

Verification: show vlan brief on a fresh switch shows every port listed under VLAN 1 'default' with status active. The line for VLAN 1 can never be absent.

hack.memory: VLAN 1 = 'the factory-default everyone's stuck in.' Every port starts here. Every trunk treats it as native. Every Cisco control protocol lives here. Three rules: move users OUT, change trunk native AWAY from 1, never use 1 for management in production.
hack.practice: Reset a switch to factory: erase startup-config, reload. Run show vlan brief — every port is under VLAN 1. Run show interfaces trunk (configure a trunk first) — Native VLAN = 1. Now create VLAN 10 and move ports: the ports disappear from VLAN 1 but VLAN 1 itself still exists. That permanence is the key takeaway.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 16 opens with the VLAN 1 default. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam loves asking 'what is the default VLAN?' (1), 'what is the default native VLAN?' (1), and 'which VLAN is used by CDP?' (1).

2.1.b.info:
VLAN 1 is permanent. The command no vlan 1 is rejected by IOS with an error along the lines of 'Default VLAN 1 may not be deleted.' You cannot remove it, shut it down from global config, or hide it. The same applies to the legacy reserved VLANs 1002-1005.

Why the restriction exists: VLAN 1 hosts several hardcoded control-plane protocols that the switch needs to function — CDP neighbor discovery, VTP advertisements (when VTP is active), PAgP EtherChannel negotiation, STP BPDUs for the default instance, and DTP trunk negotiation. If VLAN 1 could be deleted, these protocols would have nowhere to run and large chunks of switch behavior would break. Cisco chose to make VLAN 1 a permanent fixture rather than complicate the rest of the OS.

What you CAN do with VLAN 1:

  • Remove every access port from it by reassigning them: switchport access vlan [non-1]. This leaves VLAN 1 in the database but empty of user ports.
  • Prune it from trunks with switchport trunk allowed vlan remove 1. This stops user-plane traffic from crossing the trunk in VLAN 1, though control-plane frames (CDP/STP/etc.) may still flow.
  • Change the native VLAN so untagged frames don't land in VLAN 1: switchport trunk native vlan 99. This is the mitigation for VLAN hopping via double-tagging.
  • Move the management SVI off VLAN 1 by creating interface vlan 99 and shutting down interface vlan 1.

What you CANNOT do: no vlan 1 (errors), no interface vlan 1 works but the underlying VLAN stays, shutdown vlan 1 (also rejected on most platforms). VLAN 1 is load-bearing for Cisco's L2 control plane.

Exam line: 'Which VLAN cannot be deleted?' = VLAN 1. Often paired with the follow-up 'which VLANs are reserved?' = 1002-1005 (also cannot be deleted, for the Token Ring/FDDI legacy reason).

items: Hardcoded: CDP, VTP, PAgP, DTP, STP

hack.memory: VLAN 1 = 'the load-bearing wall you can't knock down.' no vlan 1 = hard error. You can empty it (move ports out, prune from trunks, change native), but the VLAN itself is there for life. Same rule: VLANs 1002-1005 (Token Ring/FDDI ghosts) — also permanent.
hack.practice: Try no vlan 1 in config mode — observe the exact error string ('Default VLAN 1 may not be deleted.'). Try no vlan 1003 — same refusal. Then demonstrate workarounds: reassign all access ports to VLAN 10, prune VLAN 1 from a trunk with switchport trunk allowed vlan remove 1, change native VLAN to 99. VLAN 1 survives all of it.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 16-17 covers the rule. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam hits this directly: memorize 'VLAN 1 and 1002-1005 cannot be deleted,' and the mitigation strategies (prune, move native, empty the VLAN).

2.1.b.info:
The management VLAN is the VLAN used for administrative access to the switch itself — SSH, Telnet, SNMP, syslog, TACACS+/RADIUS. It hosts the switch's Layer 3 presence via an SVI (Switch Virtual Interface). By default on a fresh switch this is VLAN 1, but best practice is to create a dedicated management VLAN such as VLAN 99.

Why separate management from user VLANs: (1) Security — user ports should never share a broadcast domain with the interface that accepts SSH connections to the switch. (2) Fault isolation — if a broadcast storm or loop hits a user VLAN, management is still reachable. (3) Access control — you can apply ACLs to the management SVI specifically, limiting who can reach it to a jump host or management subnet. (4) Best practice checkbox — every security framework (CIS, NIST) recommends a dedicated management VLAN.

Configuration on a Layer 2 switch:

vlan 99
 name Management
!
interface vlan 99
 ip address 10.0.99.10 255.255.255.0
 no shutdown
!
ip default-gateway 10.0.99.1

Then on any trunk and on the management-facing access port, ensure VLAN 99 is allowed. SSH config (ip domain-name, crypto key generate rsa, username, line vty 0 15 + transport input ssh + login local) rides on top.

Layer 2 vs Layer 3 switch difference: On an L2 switch, you can have exactly one active SVI, and you need ip default-gateway because the switch doesn't route. On an L3 switch, you can have many SVIs — you use a default route (ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop]) instead of ip default-gateway, and ip routing must be enabled.

Verification: show ip interface brief | include Vlan shows the management SVI and its IP. ping from the management subnet. show ip default-gateway confirms the L2 switch gateway.

children.name: Allowed on trunks, restricted by ACL

hack.memory: Management VLAN = 'the back door only the admin uses.' Dedicated VLAN (e.g., 99), dedicated SVI, dedicated gateway. Never mix with user VLANs. L2 switch uses ip default-gateway; L3 switch uses a real default route + ip routing.
hack.practice: On a 2960 (L2): create VLAN 99, interface vlan 99 with IP 10.0.99.10/24, no shutdown. Add ip default-gateway 10.0.99.1. Connect a laptop in VLAN 99 and SSH to the switch. Then try the same on a 3560 (L3): you'd omit the default-gateway and instead add ip routing + ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 10.0.99.1. Confirm both work.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 16-17 introduces the management SVI concept. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8 and Chapter 6 (Switch Management). Exam sims often require configuring a management VLAN + SVI. Know the L2 vs L3 gateway distinction cold.

2.1.b.info:
A parking-lot VLAN (also called a black-hole VLAN or unused VLAN) is a VLAN created specifically to hold unused switch ports. It has no SVI, no default gateway, and no routing to any other VLAN. A device plugged into a port in the parking-lot VLAN gets link and an IP from its own assigned subnet (if any), but has no path to the rest of the network.

The problem it solves: On a fresh switch, every unused port is in VLAN 1. If VLAN 1 has a gateway and internet access, anyone who plugs into a random jack in a conference room, closet, or unused cube gets full network access. That's a security failure — physical access to a cable shouldn't equal logical access to the network.

Standard hardening recipe:

vlan 999
 name Parking-Lot
!
interface range Gi0/5 - 48
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 999
 shutdown

Two layers of defense: (1) the port is administratively shut down, and (2) even if someone re-enables it, it's in a VLAN with nowhere to go. The parking-lot VLAN is never included on trunks, never routed, never given a gateway.

Best-practice combo: Parking-lot VLAN + shut ports + spanning-tree bpduguard enable + switchport port-security = defense in depth for unused ports. An attacker would have to: (1) unshut the port, (2) move it out of the parking-lot VLAN, (3) bypass BPDU Guard, (4) beat port-security MAC limits. Each layer is a separate tripwire.

Not the same as a management VLAN or VLAN 1. Parking-lot is specifically a black-hole dead-end. Management is a carefully controlled admin-only VLAN with tight ACLs. VLAN 1 is the default — don't use it for either purpose.

hack.memory: Parking-lot VLAN = 'the network jail for unused ports.' Number it weird (999) so it's obvious. No SVI, no routing, always shut. Plug in = no IP, no path, no fun. Combine with BPDU Guard + port-security for full defense.
hack.practice: On a 2960, create VLAN 999 named Parking-Lot. Use interface range Gi0/10 - 24 and apply: switchport mode access, switchport access vlan 999, shutdown. Try to plug a PC into one of those ports — no link light. no shutdown on one port — link comes up but the PC gets no DHCP, no gateway, no connectivity. Perfect black hole.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 mentions the concept. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8 covers it under VLAN security. The exam might ask 'how do you secure unused ports?' = assign to unused/parking-lot VLAN AND shut down. Know both halves.

2.1.b.info:
Shutting down every unused switch port is a Cisco best practice and a baseline requirement in most security frameworks. It removes the attack surface created by live-but-unused network jacks. The command is simple — shutdown on the interface — but the discipline of applying it to every unused port on every switch is what actually matters.

Why 'just disconnect' isn't enough: A physical network jack in a conference room, hallway, or empty cubicle is a permanent attack vector as long as the switch port behind it is in no shutdown. Anyone — a visitor, contractor, janitor, or attacker — can plug in a laptop or rogue device and get network access. Shutting down the port means the jack is dead: no link, no DHCP, no frames. Until an admin explicitly brings the port back up, it's a brick.

Layered hardening on unused ports: The full best-practice recipe combines several measures:

  1. shutdown — administrative down. Primary defense.
  2. switchport mode access — prevents DTP-based trunk spoofing if re-enabled.
  3. switchport access vlan 999 — move to parking-lot VLAN so even if unshut, no connectivity.
  4. spanning-tree bpduguard enable — if someone plugs in a switch, the port err-disables.
  5. switchport port-security + switchport port-security maximum 1 — limits to one MAC.

Bulk configuration pattern:

interface range Gi0/10 - 48
 description UNUSED — parking lot
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 999
 shutdown

Using interface range lets you apply the same config to dozens of ports in one pass.

Operational tradeoff: Shutting ports down means users can't self-service by just plugging into any jack — they need an admin to enable the port. That's the security/operations tradeoff. In high-security environments (CoreWeave, financial, government), ports stay shut until a ticket brings them up. In casual environments, admins sometimes skip this and rely on 802.1X instead.

hack.memory: Unused port = shut + parking VLAN + BPDU Guard + port-security. Even if one layer fails, the next one catches. Shortcut: 'SPBP' — Shut, Parking, BPDU Guard, Port-security. Never leave a port in no shutdown + VLAN 1 'just in case' — that's an open door with a welcome mat.
hack.practice: Use interface range Gi0/10 - 48 on a 2960 and apply the full recipe: shut, mode access, access vlan 999, spanning-tree bpduguard enable, switchport port-security, switchport port-security maximum 1. Verify with show interfaces status — all those ports show 'disabled.' Bring one up and plug in a switch — BPDU Guard err-disables the port within seconds. That chain of defenses in action is memorable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 16-17 (VLAN hardening) and later port-security/BPDU Guard videos cover the full chain. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 8 and 10. Exam asks 'which command disables a port?' = shutdown, and 'how do you secure unused switch ports?' = shutdown + parking-lot VLAN.

2.1.b.info:
ip default-gateway [ip-address] is a global configuration command that tells a Layer 2 switch where to send packets destined for networks other than its own directly connected subnet. It exists because L2 switches don't route — they have no routing table, no ip routing, no way to make a routing decision. The switch itself needs a gateway just like any endpoint does.

When it applies: Only on pure Layer 2 switches like the 2960/2950 series, or on multilayer switches that have NOT enabled ip routing. If ip routing is enabled (typical for 3560/3650/9300 used as L3 devices), then ip default-gateway is ignored — the switch uses the regular routing table and you configure a default route instead: ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop].

What traffic uses it: Only traffic sourced by the switch itself — SSH/Telnet sessions from the VTY to a remote host, SNMP traps, syslog, TACACS+/RADIUS auth requests, NTP queries, DNS lookups. User traffic flowing THROUGH the switch is bridged at L2 (within a VLAN) or dropped (between VLANs); it never uses ip default-gateway. This is why engineers sometimes forget to configure it — user pings work fine, but SSH from the switch to a jump host fails until you add the gateway.

Configuration context:

! Layer 2 switch (2960)
vlan 99
!
interface vlan 99
 ip address 10.0.99.10 255.255.255.0
 no shutdown
!
ip default-gateway 10.0.99.1

The gateway IP must be in the same subnet as one of the switch's SVIs (typically the management SVI). Only one default-gateway can be active at a time.

Common mistakes: (1) Configuring ip default-gateway on an L3 switch that already has ip routing — it's silently ignored, and the command looks fine in the config. (2) Gateway IP in the wrong subnet — the switch can't ARP for it, traffic drops. (3) Forgetting it entirely — management works on-subnet but dies off-subnet.

hack.memory: ip default-gateway = 'where do I (the switch) send packets that aren't on my subnet?' ONLY for L2 switches. L3 switches ignore it — they use a real default route with ip routing. Also, it's ONLY for switch-sourced traffic (SSH, SNMP, syslog) — never for user traffic passing through.
hack.practice: On a 2960: configure SVI interface vlan 99 with IP 10.0.99.10/24. Without ip default-gateway, ping 8.8.8.8 from the switch fails. Add ip default-gateway 10.0.99.1 and retry — pings succeed. Now do the same on a 3560 with ip routing on — notice ip default-gateway is ignored; you need ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 10.0.99.1 instead. This side-by-side test makes the L2-vs-L3 difference permanent.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 17 covers switch management; Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. The exam asks 'which command provides a default gateway for a Layer 2 switch?' = ip default-gateway. Be careful with the L3-switch variant — it's a common trap.

2.1.c.info:
Inter-VLAN routing (IVR) is the process of moving traffic between VLANs using a Layer 3 device. Because VLANs are Layer 2 broadcast domains, hosts in different VLANs cannot communicate at Layer 2 — even if they're plugged into the same physical switch, they might as well be in different buildings. To cross the VLAN boundary, a frame must be stripped, its IP packet routed based on destination IP, and a new frame built for the destination VLAN.

The three standard methods (ranked by real-world use):

  1. L3 switch with SVIs — most common in modern enterprise campus. Fast, cheap, integrated.
  2. Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS) — one router interface, trunk to switch, subinterfaces per VLAN. Exam favorite, still used at branch offices.
  3. Separate physical interfaces — one router port per VLAN. Simple but wasteful; mostly obsolete.

What actually happens in a packet's life: Host A in VLAN 10 (10.0.10.5) sends a packet to Host B in VLAN 20 (10.0.20.5). Because the destination is off-subnet, Host A sends the frame to its default gateway's MAC (an SVI or router subinterface). The L3 device strips the L2 header, checks the routing table (finds a connected route to 10.0.20.0/24), builds a new L2 header with source MAC = its own VLAN 20 interface and destination MAC = Host B (via ARP), and injects the frame into VLAN 20. Host B receives a normal frame; the VLAN hop is invisible above L3.

Why default gateways matter so much: Every VLAN needs a default gateway IP on the L3 device, and every host must point to that gateway. Forgetting to set gateways on hosts, or configuring the wrong IP, is the #1 cause of 'can't reach other VLANs' troubleshooting tickets. The gateway IP must live on the L3 device's SVI or subinterface for the corresponding VLAN.

Broadcast scope reminder: Routing between VLANs does not merge broadcast domains. Each VLAN remains its own broadcast domain. ARP in VLAN 10 never reaches VLAN 20 — the L3 device ARPs separately on each side. This is exactly how you want it: containment by design.

nodes: L3 device: strip L2, route, re-encap

hack.memory: IVR = 'VLANs are islands, only a L3 ferry crosses.' At L2, VLAN 10 and VLAN 20 cannot talk. At L3, a router or L3 switch ferries packets: strip L2, route at L3, re-encap L2 on the other side. Every VLAN MUST have a gateway on the L3 device, and every host MUST point to that gateway.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, put PC1 in VLAN 10 (10.0.10.5/24 GW 10.0.10.1) and PC2 in VLAN 20 (10.0.20.5/24 GW 10.0.20.1). With no L3 device, ping fails — they're isolated. Add an L3 switch with SVIs for both VLANs plus ip routing. Ping succeeds. Then remove one SVI — the other VLAN goes offline. This proves IVR is fundamentally about the L3 device.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 18 (Inter-VLAN Routing) is the canonical video. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 8-9 cover all three methods. Exam guarantees at least one IVR config sim — typically ROAS or L3 switch SVIs. Know both cold.

2.1.c.info:
Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS) is an inter-VLAN routing method where a single router interface connects to the switch via an 802.1Q trunk, and the router uses logical subinterfaces — one per VLAN — to represent each VLAN's gateway. The name comes from the visual of one router sitting on 'one stick' (cable) into the switch, juggling multiple VLANs over that single physical link.

Why it exists: Before L3 switches were cheap, the only way to route between VLANs was with an external router. Giving each VLAN its own physical router interface didn't scale (see: separate physical interfaces). ROAS solves this by multiplexing multiple VLANs over one trunk, and using subinterfaces as the logical gateway for each.

Router configuration (for VLANs 10 and 20):

interface Gi0/0
 no ip address
 no shutdown
!
interface Gi0/0.10
 encapsulation dot1q 10
 ip address 10.0.10.1 255.255.255.0
!
interface Gi0/0.20
 encapsulation dot1q 20
 ip address 10.0.20.1 255.255.255.0

Switch side: The switch port connecting to the router must be a trunk: switchport mode trunk, switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20, switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q (if the platform supports ISL too).

Key rules to remember: (1) The physical interface must be no shutdown but usually has no IP. (2) Each subinterface needs encapsulation dot1q [vlan] — this tells the router to expect/send frames tagged with that VLAN ID. (3) The subinterface number doesn't have to match the VLAN ID (Gi0/0.100 with encapsulation dot1q 10 would work), but matching them (Gi0/0.10 → VLAN 10) is strongly recommended for sanity. (4) The subinterface's IP is the default gateway for hosts in that VLAN.

Limitations vs L3 switch: All traffic between VLANs crosses the trunk twice (in on VLAN 10, out on VLAN 20) and is routed by the router CPU. Bandwidth is limited by the trunk speed, and CPU-based routing doesn't scale beyond hundreds of Mbps on typical routers. L3 switches route in ASIC at wire speed. ROAS is fine for branch offices (tens of Mbps) but unsuitable for campus cores.

children.name: Gi0/0.10 — encapsulation dot1q 10 — IP 10.0.10.1 (GW for VLAN 10)
Gi0/0.20 — encapsulation dot1q 20 — IP 10.0.20.1 (GW for VLAN 20)

hack.memory: ROAS = 'one router, one cable, many VLANs via subinterfaces.' The recipe — SENA: (S)ubinterface + (E)ncapsulation dot1q + (N)o shutdown on the parent + (A)ddress on the subinterface. Switch side = trunk allowing those VLANs. Physical interface gets no IP but must be no shutdown.
hack.practice: Classic lab: 2 PCs in VLAN 10 and VLAN 20, one L2 switch, one router connected by a single cable (trunk). Configure the router with Gi0/0.10 and Gi0/0.20 using encapsulation dot1q and per-VLAN IPs. Set the switch port as switchport mode trunk, allowed vlan 10,20. Ping across VLANs. Intentionally forget encapsulation dot1q on one subinterface — watch the corresponding VLAN go dark. Repeat until the four ROAS commands are muscle memory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 18 is THE ROAS video — lab along with him. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18 covers ROAS. The exam virtually guarantees a ROAS config sim. Biggest traps: forgetting encapsulation dot1q, putting an IP on the physical interface instead of the subinterface, or configuring the switch as access instead of trunk.

2.1.c.info:
encapsulation dot1q [vlan-id] is a subinterface-level command that tells a router: 'frames tagged with this VLAN ID belong to this subinterface, and outbound frames from this subinterface should be tagged with this VLAN ID.' It is the linchpin of Router-on-a-Stick — without it, the router has no way to map incoming tagged frames to the correct logical interface.

What it does technically: The subinterface becomes VLAN-aware. On ingress, the router reads the 802.1Q tag, matches the VID to a subinterface's encapsulation, and delivers the packet there. On egress from that subinterface, the router adds an 802.1Q tag with the configured VLAN ID before sending the frame onto the trunk. Without this command, the subinterface is non-functional — pings to its IP fail and the router logs errors about receiving tagged frames with no matching interface.

The 'native' option: encapsulation dot1q [vlan] native marks the subinterface as handling the native VLAN of the trunk. Frames in the native VLAN arrive untagged. If your trunk's native VLAN is 1 and you want the router to route VLAN 1 traffic, you'd use encapsulation dot1q 1 native. Best practice: put the native VLAN on a non-default ID (say 99), match the switch's native VLAN config, and use encapsulation dot1q 99 native on the router.

Historical note — ISL vs dot1q: Cisco's proprietary ISL (Inter-Switch Link) trunking also supports subinterface encapsulation via encapsulation isl [vlan], but ISL is obsolete and no CCNA exam today covers it. Always use dot1q.

Common mistakes: (1) Typing encapsulation dot1q without the VLAN ID — IOS rejects it. (2) Putting two subinterfaces on the same encapsulation VLAN — only the last one applied works. (3) Configuring encapsulation dot1q 10 but the switch's trunk isn't allowing VLAN 10 — no frames arrive, ARP fails.

hack.memory: encapsulation dot1q 10 = 'this subinterface handles VLAN 10 tags.' Missing this command = subinterface is deaf. Pair with ip address (gateway for that VLAN). Native VLAN case: encapsulation dot1q 99 native to handle untagged frames. Same command on router side mirrors switch native VLAN — mismatches cause CDP errors and traffic leaks.
hack.practice: Configure a ROAS router: Gi0/0.10 with encapsulation dot1q 10 and an IP. Verify ping to a VLAN 10 host works. Now remove just the encapsulation line: no encapsulation dot1q 10. Ping immediately fails even though the IP is still configured. Re-add it — pings come back. This isolates the single most important command in ROAS.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 18 shows the command in action. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam sims almost always include this command — expect to type it on at least one subinterface. Also know the native-VLAN variant for trunk native mismatch scenarios.

2.1.c.info:
The Layer 3 switch with SVI method is the modern, dominant approach to inter-VLAN routing in enterprise campus networks. An SVI (Switch Virtual Interface) is a logical Layer 3 interface tied to a VLAN — configured with interface vlan [id]. When you add an IP address to an SVI, that IP becomes the default gateway for every host in that VLAN. The L3 switch routes between SVIs internally, in hardware, at wire speed.

What makes it fast: L3 switches (Catalyst 3560/3650/3750/3850/9200/9300 and above) have ASICs — application-specific integrated circuits — that perform routing lookups and L2 rewrites in silicon. A packet moves from VLAN 10 to VLAN 20 in nanoseconds, with zero CPU involvement for steady-state forwarding. Compare to ROAS, where every inter-VLAN packet traverses the trunk twice and hits the router CPU.

Configuration (for VLANs 10 and 20):

! Global
ip routing
!
vlan 10
 name Sales
vlan 20
 name Engineering
!
interface vlan 10
 ip address 10.0.10.1 255.255.255.0
 no shutdown
!
interface vlan 20
 ip address 10.0.20.1 255.255.255.0
 no shutdown
!
interface Gi0/1
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 10
!
interface Gi0/2
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 20

Critical global command: ip routing. Without it, the SVIs exist as management interfaces but do NOT forward packets between VLANs. This is the #1 forgotten command on L3 switches and a classic exam trap.

SVI up/up conditions: An SVI is up only when (1) the VLAN exists, (2) at least one access or trunk port in that VLAN is up/up, and (3) the SVI itself is not shut down. If you create interface vlan 10 but no port is in VLAN 10, the SVI stays down. Bring up one host port in VLAN 10 and the SVI comes up.

Routed ports (bonus): You can also convert a physical switch port to a pure L3 port with no switchport + ip address. This turns a single port into a router interface (no VLAN concept). Used for L3 uplinks to other L3 devices. Separate feature from SVIs.

children.name: ASIC routes between SVIs at wire speed

hack.memory: L3 switch SVI = 'each VLAN gets a virtual interface, routed in hardware.' Recipe — VIA + IR: (V)LAN + (I)nterface vlan N + (A)ddress, and globally (I)p (R)outing. Forget ip routing and inter-VLAN dies silently. SVIs need at least one active port in the VLAN to come up.
hack.practice: On a 3560/3650 in Packet Tracer: create VLANs 10 and 20, assign access ports, create SVIs with IPs. BEFORE typing ip routing, try to ping across VLANs — fails. Add ip routing, retry — works immediately. Then shut down all ports in VLAN 10 and watch interface vlan 10 go down. Bring a port back up — SVI comes up. This teaches the three dependencies: VLAN exists, port up in VLAN, ip routing enabled.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 18 covers SVI-based IVR. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. Exam sims frequently hand you a broken L3 switch config and ask why routing isn't working — 90% of the time the answer is 'ip routing is missing.' Memorize the four must-haves: VLAN, port, SVI+IP, ip routing.

2.1.c.info:
ip routing is a global configuration command that enables Layer 3 routing functionality on a multilayer (L3) switch. Without it, an L3-capable switch behaves exactly like a Layer 2 switch — SVIs are purely for management, and packets cannot cross VLAN boundaries. It is the single most silently-dangerous forgotten command in the entire CCNA.

Why it's not on by default: On most Cisco multilayer switches (3560/3650/3850/9200/9300), ip routing is disabled by default. Cisco ships the switch ready to act as an L2 device because the most common deployment at the access layer is L2-only. If you want the switch to route, you opt in explicitly. The exception is some higher-end platforms or specific IOS images where it's pre-enabled — always verify with show running-config | include ip routing.

What it enables: (1) The switch builds a routing table (RIB) and processes packets at L3 as well as L2. (2) SVIs forward inter-VLAN traffic. (3) Routed ports (physical ports with no switchport + ip address) become functional as router interfaces. (4) Dynamic routing protocols (OSPF, EIGRP) can be configured and form neighbor relationships. (5) Static routes become meaningful.

What happens without it: You can configure SVIs with IPs, enter router ospf 1, and do everything else — IOS accepts all commands without error. But the switch silently drops anything trying to route between VLANs. The only symptom is 'hosts can't ping across VLANs,' and the diagnosis requires knowing this command exists. Engineers can lose an hour to this one omission.

Verification: show ip route on an L3 switch without ip routing shows 'Gateway of last resort is not set' and may show connected networks for SVIs, but packets between them aren't forwarded. With ip routing, show ip route behaves identically to a router — C/L entries for connected VLANs, plus any static or dynamic routes.

Default route on an L3 switch: Use ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop] to send non-local traffic to an upstream router. This replaces the ip default-gateway command used on L2 switches.

hack.memory: ip routing = 'flip the L3 switch ON.' Without it, an L3 switch is a pricy L2 switch with decorative SVIs. When inter-VLAN dies with no obvious cause, CHECK THIS FIRST. Mnemonic: 'no IR, no route' — no ip routing, no routing at all.
hack.practice: On a 3560/3650: configure VLANs, SVIs with IPs, and access ports for two VLANs. Before typing ip routing, ping between VLAN hosts — fails. Run show ip route — minimal output, no routing happening. Type ip routing in global config. Without changing anything else, retry the ping — it works. Re-run show ip route — now you see C entries for the VLAN subnets. That one moment of 'oh, THAT was the missing piece' is the exam-saving memory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 18 emphasizes ip routing. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. This command is a near-certain exam question — either as 'what's missing from this config?' or as 'what must be enabled globally for inter-VLAN routing on an L3 switch?' Answer: ip routing.

2.1.c.info:
Choosing between Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS) and L3 switch SVIs comes down to performance, hardware, and scale. The exam tests both configurations and expects you to know when each is appropriate. In the real world, L3 switches dominate campus networks while ROAS hangs on at branch offices and in labs.

Performance: L3 switches route in hardware (ASICs) at line rate — a 1 Gbps SVI forwards 1 Gbps of inter-VLAN traffic with no CPU hit. ROAS routes in software on the router CPU — limited by CPU capacity, typically hundreds of Mbps on mid-range routers, and every packet crosses the trunk twice (in on VLAN 10, out on VLAN 20), so usable bandwidth is halved.

Hardware requirements: ROAS works with any router (even cheap ISRs) plus a basic Layer 2 switch — ideal if you already have the equipment. L3 switches require an L3-capable model — 3560/3650/3750/3850/9200/9300 and up. 2960s cannot route (they're pure L2). The higher up-front cost of L3 switches is usually offset by the performance gain and reduced cable count.

Cabling and topology: ROAS uses one trunk cable between router and switch — physically clean but a single point of failure. L3 switch SVIs are internal to the switch — no external cables needed for inter-VLAN routing at all.

Scale: Adding a new VLAN with ROAS means adding a subinterface to the router AND updating the trunk's allowed VLAN list — two-step, two-device change. With L3 switch SVIs, you add a VLAN and an interface vlan X on the same device — single-step, single-device change. At scale this matters.

Feature comparison for exam recall:

AspectROASL3 Switch
Routing engineRouter CPU (software)ASIC (hardware)
Speed~100-500 Mbps typicalWire speed (1-400 Gbps)
Trunk neededYes (router ↔ switch)No (all internal)
Required commandssubif, encapsulation dot1qinterface vlan, ip routing
HardwareAny router + any switchL3-capable switch only
Typical useBranch, lab, legacyCampus, enterprise

Exam framing: 'Which IVR method is faster?' = L3 switch. 'Which works with a basic L2 switch?' = ROAS. 'Which requires ip routing globally?' = L3 switch. 'Which uses encapsulation dot1q?' = ROAS. Internalize both sides of the table.

left.label: ROAS
items: Works with any L2 switch + router

hack.memory: ROAS = old-school, cheap, slow, works everywhere. L3 switch = modern, fast, needs the right hardware. Exam decoder: 'encapsulation dot1q' = ROAS. 'ip routing' = L3 switch. 'router CPU' = ROAS. 'ASIC' = L3 switch. 'trunk to the router' = ROAS. 'SVIs' = L3 switch.
hack.practice: Build the SAME network two ways in Packet Tracer. Version 1: 2960 + router, ROAS config, trunk between them, subinterfaces on router. Version 2: 3560, SVIs with ip routing, no external router. Ping between VLANs on both. Then measure: bandwidth utilization on the trunk is doubled in ROAS (in + out). Latency is noticeably higher. This side-by-side build cements the tradeoff permanently.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 18 compares both methods. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 18. The exam loves 'which method is faster / which requires a trunk / which uses encapsulation dot1q' style questions. Lab BOTH methods at least twice. Know the command pairs cold — encapsulation dot1q for ROAS, ip routing + interface vlan for L3 switch.
